elements.sent-2411-108: This section introduces the ReinFog framework, outlining its hardware environment and software architecture.
elements.sent-2411-109: We propose a multi-layered structure that supports heterogeneous IoT, edge/fog, and cloud environments, and detail the overall architecture of our framework.
elements.sent-2411-110: ReinFog is designed to operate across a heterogeneous multi-layered hardware environment, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{hardware .}
elements.sent-2411-111: This environment encompasses three primary layers: Cloud, Edge/Fog, and IoT, each with distinct characteristics and roles in the overall system.
elements.sent-2411-112: The Cloud Layer represents the highest tier of computing resources in the ReinFog hardware environment.
elements.sent-2411-113: It consists of high-performance servers provided by different cloud service providers such as Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft Azure, and Nectar.
elements.sent-2411-114: These cloud environments offer scalable computing power and storage capabilities, high reliability and availability, and advanced services for data analytics and machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-115: The cloud layer supports containerization for consistent deployment of ReinFog components and typically handles computationally intensive tasks, large-scale data processing, and long-term data storage.
elements.sent-2411-116: The Edge/Fog Layer serves as an intermediate computing tier between the cloud and IoT devices.
elements.sent-2411-117: This layer comprises various computing devices, including laptops, desktops with varying computational capabilities, and single-board computers (e.g., Raspberry Pi).
elements.sent-2411-118: These devices are strategically positioned closer to the data source, enabling reduced latency for time-sensitive applications, local data processing and filtering, and improved privacy and security by keeping sensitive data local.
elements.sent-2411-119: The Edge/Fog layer connects to the Cloud layer via Internet connections, allowing for seamless data transfer and task offloading when needed.
elements.sent-2411-120: It also supports containerization for flexible deployment of ReinFog software components, playing a crucial role in facilitating real-time applications and reducing the computational burden on both the cloud and IoT devices.
elements.sent-2411-121: The IoT Layer comprises the various end devices and sensors that collect data and interact with the physical environment.
elements.sent-2411-122: This layer includes smart home devices (e.g., thermostats, security cameras), wearable devices, industrial sensors and actuators, connected vehicles, and environmental monitoring sensors.
elements.sent-2411-123: These devices are characterized by limited computational resources and power constraints, with direct interaction with the physical world through sensing and actuation.
elements.sent-2411-124: For connectivity, these IoT devices employ various short-range communication protocols (e.g., WiFi, Bluetooth, Zigbee, 5G) to connect with the Edge/Fog layer, while Internet-based connections facilitate communication with the Cloud layer.
elements.sent-2411-125: The IoT layer is the primary source of data in the ReinFog ecosystem, driving the need for efficient resource management and IoT application scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-126: We assume each IoT application in this layer can consist of one or multiple interdependent IoT tasks that need to be efficiently scheduled.
elements.sent-2411-127: The software architecture of ReinFog is designed to efficiently manage and schedule IoT tasks in heterogeneous edge/fog and cloud environments.
elements.sent-2411-128: As illustrated in Figure \ref{design , the framework consists of two primary subsystems, along with user interfaces for IoT application submission.}
elements.sent-2411-129: This subsystem forms the operational core of ReinFog, which is responsible for managing the execution of IoT applications across the distributed environment.. It comprises:.
elements.sent-2411-130: Master: Acting as the central coordinator, the Master manages overall system operations.
elements.sent-2411-131: It receives IoT application requests, analyzes task dependencies, and constructs directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) to represent application structures.
elements.sent-2411-132: The Master interacts with the Intelligent Task Scheduling Subsystem for optimal scheduling decisions, considering both task dependencies and resource availability.
elements.sent-2411-133: It oversees execution, ensuring correct task order.
elements.sent-2411-134: The architecture supports multiple Master instances for scalability and fault tolerance.
elements.sent-2411-135: Actor: Distributed across various nodes in the system, Actors are responsible for executing assigned tasks and managing local resources.
elements.sent-2411-136: They implement the actual task execution, monitor local system performance, and handle data transfer between tasks when necessary.
elements.sent-2411-137: Actors continuously report resource utilization, task progress, and completion status back to their associated Master.
elements.sent-2411-138: This real-time feedback enables dynamic resource management and adaptive IoT task scheduling, allowing the system to respond efficiently to changing workloads and environmental conditions.
elements.sent-2411-139: The Task Execution Subsystem handles critical functions such as application analysis, task distribution, execution monitoring, and result aggregation, ensuring efficient utilization of available resources across the heterogeneous computing environment.
elements.sent-2411-140: This subsystem represents ReinFog's core innovation, leveraging DRL to make intelligent IoT scheduling decisions.. It consists of:.
elements.sent-2411-141: Worker: Workers gather system state information from the Task Execution Subsystem and generate IoT task scheduling decisions based on the learned policy.
elements.sent-2411-142: They communicate these decisions back to the Task Execution Subsystem for implementation.
elements.sent-2411-143: Workers continuously refine their decision-making process, adapting to the system's dynamic nature through distributed learning.
elements.sent-2411-144: Learner: Centrally located, the Learner aggregates experiences or gradients from Workers and optimizes the global DRL technique.
elements.sent-2411-145: The Learner periodically distributes the refined policy to all Workers, ensuring system-wide improvement in decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-146: This continuous optimization enables ReinFog to adapt to changing conditions and enhance overall IoT task scheduling efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-147: The Intelligent Task Scheduling Subsystem continuously learns from the system's performance, adapting to changing conditions over time.
elements.sent-2411-148: This adaptive approach allows ReinFog to efficiently handle the dynamic nature of IoT workloads and the heterogeneity of computing resources.
elements.sent-2411-149: In the ReinFog framework, the workflow begins when users submit IoT applications through dedicated interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-150: These applications typically comprise multiple interdependent tasks that require coordinated execution.
elements.sent-2411-151: Upon receiving user submissions, the Master in the Task Execution Subsystem serves as the first point of contact, processing the applications by analyzing task dependencies and constructing DAGs to represent the execution structure.
elements.sent-2411-152: The Master then forwards scheduling requests to the Worker in the Intelligent Task Scheduling Subsystem, along with the system states and task characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-153: Based on this real-time information, the Worker generates IoT task scheduling decisions using DRL policies and sends these decisions back to the Master.
elements.sent-2411-154: Upon receiving these decisions, the Master coordinates its managed Actors to execute the specific tasks, with Actors handling the actual task execution and providing status feedback.
elements.sent-2411-155: Throughout this process, the Learner in the Intelligent Task Scheduling Subsystem works in parallel to optimize the DRL policies globally based on execution results and system states, continuously distributing improved policies to all Workers to enhance the system's scheduling efficiency and adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-156: This architecture allows ReinFog to balance the load across available resources and optimize overall system performance.
elements.sent-2411-157: By integrating DRL-based decision-making with traditional task execution mechanisms, ReinFog can effectively adapt to the complex and dynamic nature of modern IoT, edge/fog and cloud computing environments, providing a robust and efficient solution for resource management and IoT application scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-158: To achieve adaptive resource management in edge/fog and cloud environments, we implement the ReinFog framework by extending the core components of the FogBus2 framework and implementing and integrating the new Intelligent Task Scheduling Subsystem.
elements.sent-2411-159: We chose FogBus2 as the foundation for ReinFog because it already implements many of the basic functionalities required in our Task Execution Subsystem.
elements.sent-2411-160: FogBus2 is a lightweight and distributed container-based framework for integrating heterogeneous IoT systems with edge/fog and cloud environments.
elements.sent-2411-161: It comprises five main components that align well with our Task Execution Subsystem requirements:.
elements.sent-2411-162: User: handles environmental data collection and actuation control, similar to our user interface for IoT application submission.
elements.sent-2411-163: Master: manages IoT applications and scheduling, which forms the basis of our Master component in the Task Execution Subsystem.
elements.sent-2411-164: Actor: performs host resource profiling, aligning with our Actor component's responsibilities.
elements.sent-2411-165: Task Executor: executes submitted IoT applications, fitting directly into our execution model.
elements.sent-2411-166: Remote Logger: provides persistent log storage, supporting our system's monitoring and analysis needs.
elements.sent-2411-167: By leveraging FogBus2, we are able to focus our efforts on developing our DRL-based scheduling subsystem to handle complex, dependency-aware IoT applications.
elements.sent-2411-168: This approach allowed us to build upon a proven foundation while integrating our novel Intelligent Task Scheduling Subsystem with its DRL capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-169: The overall design of ReinFog, showing both the extended FogBus2 components and our new DRL components, is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{overview .}
elements.sent-2411-170: In this section, we present the design and organization of our DRL components: the DRL Learner and the DRL Worker.
elements.sent-2411-171: Each component is modularly designed and encompasses several sub-components and modules, which interact through well-defined APIs and internal messages.
elements.sent-2411-172: The overall design is depicted in Fig.~\ref{ddrlsys .}
elements.sent-2411-173: The DRL Learner component is responsible for training the DRL techniques and managing the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-175: The Learner DRL Core is central to policy learning and optimization.
elements.sent-2411-177: Exploration Engine: This module implements and manages exploration strategies during the training process to balance exploration and exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-178: It incorporates established methods such as $\epsilon$-greedy and Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process noise, while also providing an extensible mechanism for integrating additional exploration algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-179: The Exploration Engine exposes a well-defined API, allowing other components to interact with it efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-180: Through this API, it dynamically interacts with the Network Architecture Hub and the DRL Technique Repository via the DRL Policy Constructor, enabling it to access and utilize current technique states and network architectures.
elements.sent-2411-181: This API-driven design enhances modularity and facilitates easy integration of new exploration strategies.
elements.sent-2411-182: Network Architecture Hub: This module manages and maintains base neural networks for various DRL techniques.
elements.sent-2411-183: It supports a wide range of architectures including: \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-187: Transformers \end{itemize These diverse architectures enable the hub to accommodate different types of tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-188: The module exposes APIs for querying and updating network architectures, facilitating seamless integration and dynamic adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-189: Designed with extensibility in mind, it allows for easy incorporation of new neural network architectures and policy optimization methods in the future.
elements.sent-2411-190: DRL Technique Repository: This module efficiently manages and maintains various DRL techniques.
elements.sent-2411-191: It is divided into Centralized Techniques and Distributed Techniques sub-modules, accommodating different user requirements and computational architectures.
elements.sent-2411-192: The repository provides two integration mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-193: The native integration mechanism enables direct implementation of DRL techniques within the framework, providing optimal performance and customization capabilities for researchers to implement their own DRL techniques with full control.
elements.sent-2411-194: The library-based integration mechanism facilitates the seamless incorporation of external DRL libraries through standardized interfaces, allowing users to leverage existing implementations while reducing development time.
elements.sent-2411-195: This dual-mechanism approach provides flexibility in how DRL techniques are integrated and maintained within the framework, accommodating both custom implementations and existing libraries while ensuring consistent performance and maintainability.
elements.sent-2411-196: DRL Policy Constructor: This module is central to building and optimizing DRL policies.
elements.sent-2411-197: It orchestrates interactions with multiple modules through well-defined APIs to create and refine effective DRL strategies.
elements.sent-2411-198: The Constructor leverages the Network Architecture Hub's API for base network construction and accesses the DRL Technique Repository via API for policy development.
elements.sent-2411-199: It interacts with the Exploration Engine through its API to balance exploration and exploitation during the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-200: For policy persistence, the Constructor integrates with the Policy Manager, enabling saving and loading of policies.
elements.sent-2411-201: To enhance learning efficiency, it collaborates with the Replay Buffer for experience sampling.
elements.sent-2411-202: The DRL Policy Constructor also coordinates parallel learning processes via the Worker Session Manager and ensures policy synchronization through the Synchronizer.
elements.sent-2411-203: Policy Manager: This module is responsible for comprehensive DRL policy administration.
elements.sent-2411-204: It consists of two key sub-modules: the Saver and the Loader.
elements.sent-2411-205: The Saver sub-module ensures version-controlled, persistent storage of optimized policies in the Trained Policy Repository.
elements.sent-2411-206: The Loader sub-module, on the other hand, retrieves policies from the Repository as needed for further refinement or evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-207: The Policy Manager interfaces directly with the DRL Policy Constructor, enabling seamless integration of policy persistence operations within the overall learning process.
elements.sent-2411-208: Trained Policy Repository: This module functions as a centralized storage for optimized and validated DRL policies.
elements.sent-2411-209: It interfaces directly with the Policy Manager's Saver and Loader sub-modules, enabling efficient storage and retrieval of policies.
elements.sent-2411-210: The Repository maintains a structured archive of policies, supporting version control and rapid access for DRL experiments.
elements.sent-2411-211: It enhances the overall efficiency and effectiveness of policy management in the ReinFog framework.
elements.sent-2411-212: The Replay Buffer enhances learning efficiency and stability by storing and reusing experiences.
elements.sent-2411-213: It consists of two key modules:.
elements.sent-2411-214: Replay Buffer Warehouse: This module stores agent-environment interaction experiences.
elements.sent-2411-215: It features both random and reservoir sampling buffers.
elements.sent-2411-216: Random sampling helps break temporal correlations in the data, while reservoir sampling maintains a fixed-size buffer suitable for streaming or unbounded data.
elements.sent-2411-217: The Warehouse also supports flexible integration of additional sampling algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-218: Replay Buffer Constructor: This module manages the creation, maintenance, and updating of buffers.
elements.sent-2411-219: It interfaces with the Replay Buffer Warehouse through its API to facilitate the storage, retrieval, and sampling of experiences.
elements.sent-2411-220: It also interacts directly with the DRL Policy Constructor, supporting efficient experience utilization in the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-221: The Worker Session Manager is responsible for managing communication across multiple DRL Workers in a distributed environment.
elements.sent-2411-222: It handles thread creation, maintenance, and DRL Worker communication, ensuring fast and reliable transmission of data and instructions.
elements.sent-2411-223: This sub-component plays a crucial role in coordinating parallel learning processes and maintaining efficient distributed operations within the ReinFog framework.
elements.sent-2411-224: The Synchronizer is tasked with synchronizing training across the distributed learning environment.
elements.sent-2411-225: It coordinates policy updates, gradients, and parameters between the DRL Learner and DRL Workers, maintaining a unified learning environment.
elements.sent-2411-226: Through close collaboration with the Worker Session Manager, the Synchronizer ensures consistency in the learning process, facilitating effective distributed learning in ReinFog.
elements.sent-2411-227: The Message Handler manages inter-component communications within the ReinFog framework.
elements.sent-2411-228: It receives and processes incoming messages, efficiently routing them to the appropriate internal sub-components.
elements.sent-2411-229: It serves as a central communication hub, facilitating effective information exchange between various components of the ReinFog framework.
elements.sent-2411-230: By ensuring smooth and organized message flow, the Message Handler maintains system coherence and optimizes overall operational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-231: The DRL Worker component is responsible for interacting with the environment, collecting and processing data to support policy learning and optimization within the ReinFog framework.
elements.sent-2411-232: It shares core functionalities with the DRL Learner, with the Worker DRL Core mirroring the architecture of the Learner DRL Core to ensure consistent functionality and coordination.
elements.sent-2411-233: Despite these similarities, the DRL Worker incorporates several unique sub-components: an Environment Engine for direct interaction with the learning environment, a Trajectory Aggregator for efficient collection and processing of experience data, and a Synchronizer adapted for DRL Worker-specific synchronization tasks, functioning differently from the Synchronizer in the DRL Learner.
elements.sent-2411-234: In centralized learning scenarios, the DRL Worker can assume the role of the DRL Learner, enabling direct policy updates for DRL techniques.
elements.sent-2411-235: This comprehensive design enables flexible deployment of the DRL Worker in both centralized and distributed learning contexts, enhancing the overall adaptability and efficiency of the ReinFog framework.
elements.sent-2411-236: The Environment Engine manages interactions between the learning components and the environment through several key modules:.
elements.sent-2411-237: State Processor: This module transforms raw environmental data into a format suitable for decision-making and learning processes.
elements.sent-2411-238: Action Converter: This module translates generated actions into environment-specific commands, ensuring proper execution of decisions.
elements.sent-2411-239: Reward Generator: This module processes reward parameters and utilizes the Reward Model's API to calculate rewards.
elements.sent-2411-240: Reward Model: This module defines reward calculation methods, maintains consistency in reward generation, and supports the extension of additional reward functions.
elements.sent-2411-241: Environment Controller: This module oversees the overall interaction process, managing the flow of actions and states, and coordinating the integration of other modules.
elements.sent-2411-242: This modular design ensures a smooth and continuous interaction cycle, facilitating efficient learning and adaptation within the ReinFog framework.
elements.sent-2411-243: The Trajectory Aggregator is responsible for collecting and processing trajectories generated from interactions with the environment.
elements.sent-2411-244: These trajectories, consisting of sequences of states, actions, and rewards, encapsulate experiential data over time.
elements.sent-2411-245: The aggregator maintains a well-structured and accessible repository of these experiences for training and evaluation purposes.
elements.sent-2411-246: In specific techniques such as A3C, where gradients are calculated within DRL Workers, the Trajectory Aggregator collects and transmits these gradients to the DRL Learner for updating the global policy.
elements.sent-2411-247: Moreover, in centralized learning scenarios where policies are optimized within the DRL Worker component, the Trajectory Aggregator can adapt to function as a Replay Buffer, storing and sampling trajectory data to support policy training and updates.
elements.sent-2411-248: This versatile design enables the Trajectory Aggregator to support various learning paradigms and techniques within the ReinFog framework, enhancing flexibility and efficiency in different operational contexts.
elements.sent-2411-249: The Synchronizer in the DRL Worker maintains consistency between local and global policies in distributed learning scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-250: It periodically obtains the latest policy parameters from the DRL Learner and updates the local policies accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-251: This mechanism facilitates efficient knowledge sharing, allowing DRL Workers to make decisions based on up-to-date global knowledge while contributing to the overall learning process.
elements.sent-2411-252: The Synchronizer is essential for balancing local exploration and global exploitation within the ReinFog framework.
elements.sent-2411-253: To enable FogBus2 to work seamlessly with DRL capabilities, we have extended several of its sub-components.
elements.sent-2411-254: This section introduces these extended sub-components, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{overview .}
elements.sent-2411-255: The Scheduler \& Scaler sub-component has been extended to incorporate DRL capabilities, with the primary focus on enhancing the Scheduler Module.
elements.sent-2411-256: This extension enables coordination with the newly introduced DRL components.
elements.sent-2411-257: Fig.~\ref{extension illustrates the detailed structure of the Extended Scheduler Module within the Scheduler \& Scaler sub-component.}
elements.sent-2411-258: The DRL Component Placement Engine generates strategies for placing DRL Learners and DRL Workers.
elements.sent-2411-260: GA: Inspired by natural selection, GA evolves a population of potential solutions over generations.
elements.sent-2411-261: FA: Based on the flashing behavior of fireflies, FA uses attraction and movement towards brighter solutions.
elements.sent-2411-262: PSO: Mimicking the social behavior of bird flocking, PSO updates solutions based on personal and global best experiences.
elements.sent-2411-263: MADCP: Our proposed algorithm that combines GA, FA, and PSO to leverage their respective strengths for more efficient DRL component placement.
elements.sent-2411-264: In addition, the Engine supports the integration of additional algorithms to address diverse DRL component placement requirements and optimization objectives.
elements.sent-2411-265: The DRL Component Placement Controller retrieves placement strategies from the DRL Component Placement Engine and applies them to place DRL Learners and DRL Workers.
elements.sent-2411-266: This sub-module ensures that the DRL components are properly set up according to the generated strategies, facilitating the efficient start of the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-267: The Task Scheduling Decision Generator communicates with DRL components to obtain IoT task scheduling decisions.
elements.sent-2411-268: It then manages the deployment of these tasks based on the received decisions.
elements.sent-2411-269: This sub-module bridges the gap between DRL components and practical task execution within the ReinFog framework.
elements.sent-2411-270: The Profiler has been extended in both the Master and Actor components to provide more comprehensive system monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-271: In the Master, the extended Profiler now collects and analyzes system-wide performance metrics, providing a holistic view of the entire distributed environment.
elements.sent-2411-272: This includes monitoring resource utilization patterns, network latencies, and overall system throughput.
elements.sent-2411-273: In the Actor, the Profiler has been augmented to gather more detailed node-specific information, such as CPU and memory usage, task execution times, and local network conditions.
elements.sent-2411-274: These enhancements enable more accurate modeling of the system state, which is crucial for the DRL components to make informed IoT task scheduling decisions.
elements.sent-2411-275: The User component's Sensor and Actuator have been extended to enhance data collection and performance monitoring capabilities, supporting the integration of DRL in ReinFog.
elements.sent-2411-276: These extensions focus on gathering more detailed information about IoT device operations and application performance.
elements.sent-2411-277: Key enhancements include the ability to monitor detailed energy consumption patterns of IoT devices, fine-grained operational timing such as task execution and completion times, as well as device-specific characteristics like processing capabilities and storage capacity.
elements.sent-2411-278: Additionally, the extensions provide real-time data on network conditions and connectivity status of IoT devices.
elements.sent-2411-279: ReinFog supports both centralized and distributed deployment for DRL techniques.
elements.sent-2411-280: This section details how ReinFog components are utilized in these two deployment patterns, with specific examples to illustrate the implementation of each deployment.
elements.sent-2411-281: Centralized DRL techniques deployment is essential in ReinFog for scenarios where the environment complexity is moderate and the system can benefit from simplified architecture and reduced communication overhead.
elements.sent-2411-282: In centralized deployment, a single DRL Worker handles both learning and decision-making processes, suitable for scenarios with relatively stable workload patterns or when system resources are limited.
elements.paragraph-2411-011: Centralized DRL Deployment Scheme in ReinFog:
elements.sent-2411-283: In centralized deployment, the learning process is conducted directly within the DRL Worker, eliminating the need for a separate DRL Learner.
elements.sent-2411-284: The Environment Engine collects environmental data from the ReinFog Master and processes them for training.
elements.sent-2411-285: The Trajectory Aggregator will assume the role of the Replay Buffer, which stores and samples experiences for policy optimization.
elements.sent-2411-286: The Worker DRL Core contains all the necessary modules for both learning and decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-287: This design centralizes all environmental data collection and policy updating within the DRL Worker, creating a self-contained unit that operates independently.
elements.sent-2411-288: To demonstrate the centralized deployment, we utilize DQN \cite{mnih2015human as an example.}
elements.sent-2411-289: DQN is a widely applied DRL technique that combines Q-learning with DNNs, employing a primary network for action selection and a separate target network for value estimation to stabilize training and improve learning efficiency in sequential decision-making problems \cite{arulkumaran2017deep .}
elements.sent-2411-290: In our implementation, the DRL Worker's Environment Engine receives environmental data from the ReinFog Master as a scheduling request and processes them into suitable formats for DQN utilization.
elements.sent-2411-291: The Worker DRL Core maintains two neural networks: the primary network for action selection and the target network for stable Q-value estimation.
elements.sent-2411-292: Actions are selected based on the current Q-values and sent to the Master for IoT task scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-293: The scheduling results and rewards are processed by the Environment Engine and stored in the Trajectory Aggregator.
elements.sent-2411-294: The Worker DRL Core then samples mini-batches of experiences directly from the Trajectory Aggregator and performs Q-learning updates.
elements.sent-2411-295: Distributed DRL techniques deployment is essential in ReinFog for addressing complex, large-scale environments where centralized approaches may be inefficient.
elements.sent-2411-296: This deployment pattern can harness the computational capabilities of heterogeneous edge/fog and cloud resources through multiple DRL Workers collaborating with a single or multiple DRL Learners, enabling parallel training and distributed learning to deal with dynamic workloads that demand rapid adaptation.
elements.paragraph-2411-013: Distributed DRL Deployment Scheme in ReinFog:
elements.sent-2411-297: In distributed deployment, ReinFog employs multiple DRL Workers collaborating with a single or multiple DRL Learners to enable parallel training and decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-298: Each DRL Worker operates independently, with its Environment Engine collecting and processing environmental data from the Master and its DRL Core generating local decisions based on the current policy.
elements.sent-2411-299: The Trajectory Aggregator in each DRL Worker collects and processes local experiences for transmission.
elements.sent-2411-300: The Worker Session Manager coordinates the communication flow, enabling DRL Workers to transmit their processed experiences to the central DRL Learner.
elements.sent-2411-301: The DRL Learner stores these experiences in its Replay Buffer.
elements.sent-2411-302: The Learner DRL Core then performs global policy optimization using experiences stored and sampled from the Replay Buffer.
elements.sent-2411-303: After policy updates, the Synchronizer ensures policy consistency by distributing the latest global policy to all DRL Workers.
elements.sent-2411-304: This distributed architecture enables scalable learning across multiple nodes while maintaining policy coherence.
elements.sent-2411-305: To illustrate the distributed deployment, we present IMPALA as an example.
elements.sent-2411-306: IMPALA is a highly scalable distributed DRL technique that efficiently handles large-scale learning by employing multiple actors running in parallel with a centralized learner, using importance sampling and V-trace off-policy correction to maintain stability in the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-307: In our implementation of IMPALA, multiple DRL Workers operate in parallel, each with its Environment Engine processing environmental data received from the ReinFog Master.
elements.sent-2411-308: The Worker DRL Core maintains local copies of the actor and critic networks for policy representation.
elements.sent-2411-309: Based on these local networks, the Worker DRL Core generates actions for IoT task scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-310: The scheduling results and rewards are processed locally by the Environment Engine, and each DRL Worker's Trajectory Aggregator collects these experiences.
elements.sent-2411-311: The collected experiences are then transmitted to the DRL Learner through worker sessions managed by the Worker Session Manager, and stored in the Replay Buffer.
elements.sent-2411-312: The Learner DRL Core maintains the global actor and critic networks for policy optimization.
elements.sent-2411-313: It samples experiences from the Replay Buffer, computes importance sampling ratios to address the discrepancy between behavior and target policies, and employs V-trace for off-policy correction to update these global networks.
elements.sent-2411-314: The updated global policy parameters are then distributed to all DRL Workers through the Synchronizer, ensuring consistency across the distributed system.
elements.sent-2411-315: ReinFog supports two primary mechanisms for integrating DRL techniques: native integration and library-based integration.
elements.sent-2411-316: The native integration mechanism enables direct implementation of DRL techniques within the framework, offering optimal performance and customization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-317: The library-based integration mechanism allows seamless incorporation of external DRL libraries, providing access to established implementations while maintaining framework compatibility.
elements.sent-2411-318: This dual integration approach ensures that ReinFog can accommodate diverse research needs while leveraging existing DRL solutions.
elements.sent-2411-319: In this section, we detail both integration mechanisms and provide examples of their implementation within ReinFog.
elements.sent-2411-320: The native integrationmechanism is essential to ReinFog as it offers substantial advantages in performance, maintainability, and flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-321: Directly implementing DRL techniques within the framework can optimize performance, particularly in dynamic edge/fog and cloud environments where real-time responsiveness is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-322: This mechanism also minimizes dependencies on external libraries, enhancing stability and compatibility across various deployment scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-323: Furthermore, native integration provides researchers with the flexibility to implement and integrate their own custom DRL techniques, allowing for tailored solutions to specific research problems.
elements.sent-2411-324: To support native integration of DRL techniques, ReinFog provides a comprehensive framework through well-defined interfaces and modular design, to offer maximum flexibility while maintaining consistency and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-325: The Network Architecture Hub is responsible for neural network architecture definition and management.
elements.sent-2411-326: It maintains a collection of base network architectures (e.g., DNNs, RNNs, LSTMs, Transformers) and allows researchers to define custom architectures through a consistent interface.
elements.sent-2411-327: Building upon these network architectures, the DRL Technique Repository is responsible for managing DRL techniques, providing an extensible interface through a base class.
elements.sent-2411-328: This base class defines essential abstract methods including policy network initialization, action selection, loss computation, and parameter updates.
elements.sent-2411-329: These abstractions enable researchers to implement new DRL techniques.
elements.sent-2411-330: ReinFog's Exploration Engine supports native integration through a pluggable exploration strategy interface, offering built-in configurability for common strategies (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy and Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process noise) while enabling custom strategy implementation.
elements.sent-2411-331: The Replay Buffer enables custom buffer implementation through a standard interface.
elements.sent-2411-332: It provides several built-in buffer types (e.g., random sampling and reservoir sampling) while allowing researchers to define flexible sampling strategies and integrate specialized buffer types.
elements.sent-2411-333: To demonstrate the native integration mechanism, we describe how to implement a customized version of IMPALA that leverages ReinFog's advanced features.
elements.sent-2411-334: The Network Architecture Hub is utilized to construct two Transformer-based neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-335: The actor network processes the input states and outputs action probabilities for IoT task scheduling decisions, while the critic network evaluates these states to guide the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-336: IMPALA's core algorithm is implemented by extending the base class in the DRL Technique Repository, where the V-trace off-policy correction and importance sampling calculations are defined.
elements.sent-2411-337: The Exploration Engine is configured to use Ornstein-Uhlenbeck noise for action exploration.
elements.sent-2411-338: The Replay Buffer is set up with reservoir sampling to efficiently store and manage experiences.
elements.sent-2411-339: Through these configurations and implementations, IMPALA operates as a fully functional distributed DRL technique within the ReinFog framework.
elements.sent-2411-340: The library-based integration mechanism serves as another crucial feature of ReinFog, offering development efficiency, algorithmic diversity, and research flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-341: By importing well-established DRL libraries, researchers can leverage proven algorithms without reimplementation, significantly reducing development time while ensuring reliable performance.
elements.sent-2411-342: This mechanism also enriches ReinFog with diverse DRL techniques, enabling it to handle various scheduling scenarios in heterogeneous edge/fog and cloud environments.
elements.sent-2411-343: Moreover, it empowers researchers to integrate and experiment with different DRL libraries based on their specific research requirements.
elements.sent-2411-344: To support external DRL libraries integration, ReinFog provides a standardized interface through the DRL Technique Repository.
elements.sent-2411-345: This interface defines essential methods for bridging external libraries with ReinFog's environment.
elements.sent-2411-346: Specifically, the interface allows researchers to implement state preprocessing to convert ReinFog's system state representations into formats compatible with external libraries, action translation to map library-generated actions back to ReinFog's scheduling decisions, and reward signal adaptation to ensure proper learning feedback.
elements.sent-2411-347: The DRL Technique Repository manages these transformations, enabling external DRL techniques to operate seamlessly within ReinFog's resource management framework while maintaining their original implementations.
elements.sent-2411-348: To demonstrate the library-based integration mechanism, we provide an example of integrating Recurrent Replay Distributed DQN (R2D2) \cite{kapturowski2018recurrent from the Ray\footnote{https://www.ray.io/ library.}}
elements.sent-2411-349: Ray library is chosen for its high-performance distributed computing features and comprehensive DRL techniques suite.
elements.sent-2411-350: R2D2 is a distributed DRL technique that extends DQN by incorporating RNNs and a replay system to handle partial observability and temporal dependencies in sequential decision-making problems.
elements.sent-2411-351: Through the interface provided in the DRL Technique Repository, we implement state preprocessing to convert ReinFog's scheduling environment states (e.g., node resources, IoT task characteristics, and network conditions) into R2D2's required tensor format.
elements.sent-2411-352: The action translation mechanism transforms R2D2’s output probabilities into concrete scheduling decisions within ReinFog, effectively guiding the scheduling of IoT tasks across available nodes.
elements.sent-2411-353: For reward signal adaptation, the implementation processes ReinFog's performance metrics (e.g., scheduling result, response time, energy consumption) into a scalar reward value suitable for R2D2's learning process.
elements.sent-2411-354: These implementations enable R2D2 to effectively learn and make scheduling decisions within ReinFog while preserving its original recurrent replay-based learning mechanism.
elements.section-2411-005: MADCP: A Memetic Algorithm for DRL Component Placement
elements.sent-2411-355: In ReinFog, effective DRL-based IoT application scheduling requires careful placement of various components (e.g., DRL Learners and DRL Workers) across different nodes in the heterogeneous computing environment.
elements.sent-2411-356: Poor DRL component placement decisions can lead to increased communication overhead, inefficient resource utilization, and degraded learning performance.
elements.sent-2411-357: To solve this challenge, ReinFog offers a mechanism for DRL component placement.
elements.sent-2411-358: While traditional meta-heuristic algorithms could be applied to this placement problem, they show specific limitations.
elements.sent-2411-359: GA provides robust exploration capabilities but may converge slowly in complex solution spaces \cite{renders1996hybrid .}
elements.sent-2411-360: FA excels at local search refinement but can be trapped in local optima \cite{wu2020improved .}
elements.sent-2411-361: PSO offers efficient global search but may lack fine-tuning abilities in local regions \cite{moradi2016hybrid .}
elements.sent-2411-362: To address this critical placement challenge while overcoming these algorithmic limitations, we propose MADCP, a Memetic Algorithm for DRL Component Placement that combines the strengths of GA, FA, and PSO.
elements.sent-2411-363: In this section, we first define the optimization problem we are addressing along with the MADCP.
elements.sent-2411-364: We also explain how our proposed MADCP combines the strengths of three methods: GA, FA, and PSO.
elements.sent-2411-365: In addition, we present a comprehensive analysis of the computational complexity of MADCP, examining its initialization and iterative optimization phases.
elements.sent-2411-366: The MADCP is designed to address a complex optimization problem in distributed computing environments.
elements.sent-2411-367: The primary goal is to efficiently place DRL components across heterogeneous computing nodes to optimize overall system performance.
elements.sent-2411-368: Consider a set of DRL components $\mathcal{C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$ that need to be assigned to a set of heterogeneous computing nodes $\mathcal{N = \{N_1, N_2, \dots, N_n\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-369: Each node has different computational capabilities, memory sizes, and energy consumption rates.
elements.sent-2411-370: Each component $C_i$ has a computational requirement $U_i$ (e.g., CPU cycles), a memory requirement $M_i$, and a deadline or time constraint $D_i$.
elements.sent-2411-371: Similarly, each node $N_j$ is characterized by a computational capacity $P_j$, and available memory $A_j$.
elements.sent-2411-372: The objective is to find an optimal assignment of DRL components to computing nodes that minimizes the total operation time and energy consumption while meeting all time and resource constraints.
elements.sent-2411-373: We define the objective function as: \begin{equation}Minimize \quad F = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \big( \omega_1 \cdot O(C_i, N_j) \notag + \omega_2 \cdot E(C_i, N_j) \big),\end{equation} Here, $x_{ij}$ is a binary variable indicating whether component $C_i$ is assigned to node $N_j$: \begin{equation}x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1, \text{if component } C_i \text{ is assigned to node } N_j, 0, \text{otherwise}. \end{cases}\end{equation} $O(C_i, N_j)$ is the operation time of component $C_i$ on node $N_j$, $E(C_i, N_j)$ is the energy consumed by node $N_j$ during the operation time $O(C_i, N_j)$, and $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ are weighting factors balancing the importance of operation time and energy consumption.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}Minimize \quad F = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \big( \omega_1 \cdot O(C_i, N_j) \notag + \omega_2 \cdot E(C_i, N_j) \big),\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}x_{ij} =  1, if component  C_i  is assigned to node  N_j, 0, otherwise. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-374: The optimization problem is subject to the following constraints:.
elements.sent-2411-375: For each node $N_j$, the total computational and memory requirements of the assigned components should not exceed its capacity: \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} U_i \leq P_j, \quad \forall N_j \in \mathcal{N},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} M_i \leq A_j, \quad \forall N_j \in \mathcal{N}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-376: Each component must complete the operation within its deadline: \begin{equation}O(C_i, N_j) \leq D_i, \quad \forall C_i \in C, \notag \quad \forall N_j \in N  where  x_{ij} = 1.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}O(C_i, N_j) \leq D_i, \quad \forall C_i \in C, \notag \quad \forall N_j \in N  where  x_{ij} = 1.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-377: Each component is assigned to exactly one node: \begin{equation}x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}, \quad \forall C_i \in C, \quad \forall N_j \in N, \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall C_i \in C.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}, \quad \forall C_i \in C, \quad \forall N_j \in N, \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall C_i \in C.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-378: Combining the objective function and the constraints, the optimization problem can be formulated as: \begin{equation}Minimize \quad F = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \Big( \omega_1 \cdot O(C_i, N_j) \notag \quad\quad\quad\quad + \omega_2 \cdot E(C_i, N_j) \Big) Subject to \quad \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} U_i \leq P_j, \quad \forall N_j \in N \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} M_i \leq A_j, \quad \forall N_j \in N O(C_i, N_j) \leq D_i, \quad \forall C_i \in C, \notag \qquad\quad \forall N_j \in N  where  x_{ij} = 1 x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}, \quad \forall C_i \in C, \quad \forall N_j \in N \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall C_i \in C\end{equation} This problem belongs to a class of combinatorial optimization challenges, where the objective is to efficiently allocate resources under multiple constraints, making it computationally intensive (NP-hard).
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}Minimize \quad F = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \Big( \omega_1 \cdot O(C_i, N_j) \notag \quad\quad\quad\quad + \omega_2 \cdot E(C_i, N_j) \Big) Subject to \quad \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} U_i \leq P_j, \quad \forall N_j \in N \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} M_i \leq A_j, \quad \forall N_j \in N O(C_i, N_j) \leq D_i, \quad \forall C_i \in C, \notag \qquad\quad \forall N_j \in N  where  x_{ij} = 1 x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}, \quad \forall C_i \in C, \quad \forall N_j \in N \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall C_i \in C\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-379: The complexity arises from the exponential number of possible component-to-node assignments as the number of components and nodes increases.
elements.sent-2411-380: Traditional exact algorithms become impractical for large-scale instances because they require prohibitive computational time to explore all possible solutions.
elements.sent-2411-381: Moreover, single-method heuristic algorithms often fail to adequately balance exploration and exploitation in the vast solution space, leading to suboptimal results.
elements.sent-2411-382: Given the limitations of existing methods in addressing large-scale, NP-hard optimization problems, we propose a novel memetic algorithm named MADCP to find near-optimal solutions within reasonable computational time for DRL components (e.g., DRL Learners and DRL Workers) placement.
elements.sent-2411-383: MADCP synergistically combines the strengths of three optimization algorithms:.
elements.sent-2411-384: GA: Provides robust exploration capabilities by simulating the process of natural selection, allowing the algorithm to broadly traverse the solution space and maintain genetic diversity among solutions.
elements.sent-2411-385: FA: Enhances local search by modeling the flashing behavior of fireflies, enabling fine-tuning in promising areas and improving exploitation of the solution space.
elements.sent-2411-386: PSO: Contributes efficient global optimization and rapid convergence by simulating social behavior patterns of organisms, allowing particles (solutions) to adjust their positions based on personal and global best experiences.
elements.sent-2411-387: By integrating these algorithms, MADCP achieves a balanced search strategy that mitigates the shortcomings of individual techniques, leading to improved performance in solving complex optimization problems.
elements.sent-2411-388: Algorithm~\ref{alg:MADCP provides a detailed description of MADCP.}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{MADCP} \label{alg:MADCP} \SetAlgoLined \KwIn{ $compParameters$ -- parameters defining the components $nodeParameters$ -- parameters defining the nodes $populationSize$ -- number of individuals in the population $generations$ -- number of iterations $numOperations$ -- number of genetic operations per generation $mutationRate$ -- probability of mutation $FAParameters$ -- parameters $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ for FA $PSOParameters$ -- parameters $(w, c_1, c_2)$ for PSO } \KwOut{Optimal assignment of components to nodes} \tcc{Initialization} $components \leftarrow$ CreateComponents($compParameters$)\; $nodes \leftarrow$ CreateNodes($nodeParameters$)\; $population \leftarrow$ GeneratePopulation($populationSize$, $components$, $nodes$)\; $velocities \leftarrow$ InitializeVelocities($populationSize$, $components$)\; $personalOpt \leftarrow population$\; $globalOpt \leftarrow \arg\max_{p \in population}$ CalculateFitness($p$, $components$)\; \For{$i \leftarrow 1$ \KwTo $generations$}{ \tcc{Fitness Evaluation} \ForEach{$p \in population$}{ $fitnessValues \leftarrow$ CalculateFitness($p$, $components$)\; } $newPopulation \leftarrow \emptyset$\; \tcc{Genetic Algorithm Operations} \For{$j \leftarrow 1$ \KwTo $numOperations$}{ \tcc{Selection} $parent1, parent2 \leftarrow$ SelectParents($population$, $fitnessValues$)\; \tcc{Crossover} $child1, child2 \leftarrow$ Crossover($parent1$, $parent2$)\; \tcc{Mutation} $child1 \leftarrow$ Mutate($child1$, $mutationRate$, $nodes$)\; $child2 \leftarrow$ Mutate($child2$, $mutationRate$, $nodes$)\; Add $child1$ and $child2$ to $newPopulation$\; } $population \leftarrow newPopulation$\; \tcc{Firefly Algorithm Movement} $population \leftarrow$ FireflyMovement($population$, $components$, $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$)\; \tcc{Particle Swarm Optimization Update} $population$, $velocities \leftarrow$ PSOUpdate($population$, $velocities$, $personalOpt$, $globalOpt$, $w$, $c_1$, $c_2$)\; \tcc{Update Personal and Global Best Solutions} \ForEach{$p \in population$}{ \If{CalculateFitness($p$, $components$) $>$ CalculateFitness($personalOpt$, $components$)}{ $personalOpt \leftarrow p$\; } } $globalOpt \leftarrow$ UpdateGlobalOpt($population$, $globalOpt$, $components$)\; } \Return $globalOpt$\;
elements.sent-2411-389: In the initialization phase (Lines 1--6), the algorithm begins by generating the set of components and nodes based on the provided parameters.
elements.sent-2411-390: The function CreateComponents initializes the components $\mathcal{C}$, each with specific computational and memory requirements, as well as deadlines.
elements.sent-2411-391: Similarly, CreateNodes initializes the nodes $\mathcal{N}$, each characterized by their computational capacities and available memory.
elements.sent-2411-392: An initial population of candidate solutions is generated using GeneratePopulation, where each individual represents a potential assignment of components to nodes.
elements.sent-2411-393: For the PSO phase, velocities for each individual are initialized using InitializeVelocities.
elements.sent-2411-394: The personal best solutions $personalOpt$ are initially set to the current population, and the global best solution $globalOpt$ is determined by evaluating the fitness of each individual using CalculateFitness and selecting the one with the highest fitness value.
elements.sent-2411-395: The algorithm then enters the main loop (Lines 7--28), iterating over a predefined number of generations to evolve the population towards optimality.
elements.sent-2411-396: At the beginning of each generation, the fitness of each individual in the population is calculated using CalculateFitness (Lines 8--10).
elements.sent-2411-397: The fitness function is designed based on the objective function defined in the optimization problem, considering execution time and energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-398: In the Genetic Algorithm operations (Lines 12--18), selection, crossover, and mutation are performed to generate a new population.
elements.sent-2411-399: Pairs of parent individuals are selected from the current population based on their fitness values using SelectParents (Line 13).
elements.sent-2411-400: The selected parents undergo crossover using Crossover (Line 14) to produce offspring, combining parts of the parents' solutions to create new individuals and promote exploration of the solution space.
elements.sent-2411-401: The offspring are then subjected to mutation using Mutate (Lines 15--16), introducing random changes to some genes (component assignments) with a probability defined by the mutation rate.
elements.sent-2411-402: This helps maintain genetic diversity and prevents premature convergence.
elements.sent-2411-403: The newly created children are added to form a new population (Line 19), replacing the old one.
elements.sent-2411-404: After the Genetic Algorithm operations, the Firefly Algorithm is applied to the population using FireflyMovement (Line 20).
elements.sent-2411-405: Each individual moves towards better solutions based on attractiveness and randomization parameters, enhancing local search by exploiting promising regions in the solution space.
elements.sent-2411-406: Subsequently, the Particle Swarm Optimization phase updates the positions and velocities of individuals using PSOUpdate (Line 21).
elements.sent-2411-407: Velocities are updated based on the inertia weight, cognitive coefficient, and social coefficient, guiding individuals towards their personal best and the global best solutions.
elements.sent-2411-408: Positions are then updated based on the new velocities, contributing to global optimization and convergence of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-409: The personal best solutions $personalOpt$ are updated by comparing the current fitness of each individual with their personal best fitness (Lines 22--26); if an individual has achieved a better fitness, its personal best is updated accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-410: The global best solution $globalOpt$ is updated by identifying the best individual in the current population based on fitness evaluations (Line 27).
elements.sent-2411-411: After completing all generations, the algorithm returns the global best solution $globalOpt$ (Line 29), which represents the optimal assignment of components to nodes found by MADCP.
elements.sent-2411-412: This section provides a detailed analysis of the computational complexity of the proposed MADCP.
elements.sent-2411-413: The complexity analysis is divided into two main parts: the initialization phase and the iterative optimization phase.
elements.sent-2411-414: By analyzing the time complexity of each phase, we derive the overall complexity of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-415: The initialization phase consists of several operations, including the creation of components and nodes, population generation, velocity initialization, and initial fitness evaluation:.
elements.sent-2411-416: Component and Node Creation: The components and nodes are created based on the input parameters, where $M$ represents the number of components, and $N$ denotes the number of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-417: The complexity of this step is $O(M)$ for component creation and $O(N)$ for node creation, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-418: Population Generation: The initial population, with size $P$, is generated based on the number of components and nodes.
elements.sent-2411-419: Because each individual in the population represents a mapping between components and nodes, the complexity of this step is $O(P \times M)$.
elements.sent-2411-420: Velocity Initialization: The initial velocity of each individual is set based on the number of individuals in the population.
elements.sent-2411-421: The velocity initialization is performed by iterating over each component, so the complexity of this step is $O(P \times M)$.
elements.sent-2411-422: Fitness Evaluation: The initial fitness of each individual in the population is calculated based on component assignments.
elements.sent-2411-423: The complexity for calculating the fitness of a single individual is $O(M)$.
elements.sent-2411-424: Therefore, evaluating the fitness of the entire population results in a complexity of $O(P \times M)$.
elements.sent-2411-425: Combining the above components, the total complexity of the initialization phase can be expressed as: \begin{equation}O(M + N + P \times M).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-426: The iterative optimization phase consists of fitness evaluation, GA operations (selection, crossover, and mutation), FA-based movement, PSO update, and the update of personal and global best solutions.
elements.sent-2411-427: Fitness Evaluation: In each generation, the fitness of every individual in the population is evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-428: The time complexity for evaluating the fitness of the entire population is $O(P \times M)$.
elements.sent-2411-429: Genetic Algorithm Operations: This step includes parent selection, crossover, and mutation: \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-430: Selection: Assuming a selection mechanism such as roulette wheel selection, the complexity is $O(P)$.
elements.sent-2411-431: Crossover: The crossover operation generates two offspring, with a complexity of $O(M)$.
elements.sent-2411-432: Mutation: Each offspring undergoes mutation with a complexity of $O(M)$.
elements.sent-2411-433: Assume the GA operations are performed $P/2$ times per generation, the total complexity for GA operations is: \begin{equation}O\left(\frac{P}{2} \times (P + 2 \times M)\right) = O\left(P^2 + P \times M\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-434: The complexity of this step is $O(P^2 \times M)$.
elements.sent-2411-435: The complexity of this step is $O(P \times M)$.
elements.sent-2411-436: The complexity for updating the personal and global best solutions is $O(P \times M)$.
elements.sent-2411-437: Assume the number of generations is $G$, the total complexity of the iterative optimization phase is: \begin{equation}O(G \times P^2 \times M).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-438: By combining the complexity of the initialization phase and the iterative optimization phase, the overall time complexity of the MADCP is: \begin{equation}O(M + N + P \times M + G \times P^2 \times M).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}O(M + N + P \times M + G \times P^2 \times M).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-439: In most practical scenarios, the complexity of the iterative optimization phase $O(G \times P^2 \times M)$ dominates the overall complexity, especially for larger values of $G$, $P$, or $M$.
elements.sent-2411-440: Therefore, the overall time complexity of the MADCP can be expressed as: \begin{equation}O(G \times P^2 \times M).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-441: The complexity analysis shows that although MADCP combines GA, FA, and PSO, the overall complexity does not significantly increase.
elements.sent-2411-442: Specifically, GA’s selection, crossover, and mutation operations have a complexity of $O(P^2 + P \times M)$, and PSO’s update operations have a complexity of $O(P \times M)$, both of which are relatively low and do not heavily impact the total complexity.
elements.sent-2411-443: The FA’s movement operation, with a complexity of $O(P^2 \times M)$, becomes the dominant factor as the population size increases.
elements.sent-2411-444: Thus, the total complexity of MADCP remains $O(G \times P^2 \times M)$, which is consistent with the complexity of using FA alone and does not increase significantly due to the addition of GA and PSO.
elements.sent-2411-445: Instead, MADCP leverages GA to enhance population diversity, preventing premature convergence; FA to improve local search capabilities; and PSO to guide global convergence.
elements.sent-2411-446: This results in a superior optimization performance without adding substantial computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-447: In essence, MADCP achieves a balanced integration of the strengths of each algorithm while maintaining manageable complexity, making it a highly effective optimization algorithm for complex optimization problems.
elements.sent-2411-448: This section presents a comprehensive evaluation of ReinFog's performance in addressing the complex IoT application scheduling problem across heterogeneous edge and cloud environments.
elements.sent-2411-449: In our performance evaluation, we adhered to established benchmarking practices to ensure the reliability of our results.
elements.sent-2411-450: We repeated each experiment 10 times per configuration to account for the variability in measurements.
elements.sent-2411-451: Moreover, we carefully controlled the experimental environment to minimize external disturbances and ensure consistent conditions for all tests.
elements.sent-2411-452: The final results presented are the averages of these repeated experiments, ensuring that they accurately reflect the typical system performance.
elements.sent-2411-453: We conduct our experiments in a heterogeneous computing environment comprising cloud, edge/fog, and IoT layers.
elements.sent-2411-454: The cloud layer consists of a multi-cloud setup including one AWS VM (Intel Xeon, 1 core @ 2.4GHz, 1GB RAM), one Azure VM (Intel Xeon, 1 core @ 2.3GHz, 1GB RAM), and 30 Nectar Cloud VMs (AMD EPYC; 16 VMs with 2 cores @ 2.0GHz, 8GB RAM; 10 VMs with 4 cores @ 2.0GHz, 16GB RAM; 4 VMs with 8 cores @ 2.0GHz, 32GB RAM).
elements.sent-2411-455: The edge/fog layer consists of an Apple Macbook Pro (macOS, Apple M1 Pro, 8 cores, 16GB RAM), a Dell laptop (Linux, Intel Core i7, 8 cores @ 2.3GHz, 16GB RAM), and a Raspberry Pi 3B (Raspberry Pi OS, Broadcom BCM2837, 4 cores @ 1.2GHz, 1GB RAM).
elements.sent-2411-456: Devices in the IoT layer are equipped with 2 cores @ 3.2GHz and 4GB RAM.
elements.sent-2411-457: The network configuration is characterized by the following latency/bandwidth metrics: IoT to Nectar 8-12ms/14-18MB/s, IoT to AWS 16-22ms/16-20MB/s, IoT to Azure 8-15ms/15-22MB/s, and IoT to edge/fog 1-4ms/130-150MB/s.
elements.sent-2411-458: We deploy four IoT applications in our experiments: Face Detection, Color Tracking, Face and Eye Detection, and Video Optical Character Recognition.
elements.sent-2411-459: These applications feature adjustable resolution and support both real-time and non-real-time processing.
elements.sent-2411-460: Among the implemented/integrated DRL techniques, we use the following representative techniques to conduct our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-461: The configuration of each technique is discussed below:.
elements.sent-2411-462: IMPALA \cite{espeholt2018impala : IMPALA is a distributed DRL technique natively supported by ReinFog.}
elements.sent-2411-463: We choose it for its exceptional scalability and efficiency in handling distributed learning scenarios \cite{espeholt2018impala .}
elements.sent-2411-464: In our experiments, it uses Ornstein–Uhlenbeck noise exploration strategy and the reservoir sampling replay buffer, and its network structure introduces Transformers.
elements.sent-2411-465: The DRL component placement algorithm is set to MADCP.
elements.sent-2411-466: A3C \cite{pmlr-v48-mniha16 : A3C is a distributed DRL technique natively supported by ReinFog.}
elements.sent-2411-467: We choose it for its asynchronous parallel learning architecture that enables efficient policy optimization through multiple independent actors operating concurrently \cite{pmlr-v48-mniha16 .}
elements.sent-2411-468: In our experiments, it uses $\epsilon$-greedy exploration strategy, and its network structure introduces LSTMs.
elements.sent-2411-469: The DRL component placement algorithm is set to FA.
elements.sent-2411-470: PPO \cite{schulman2017proximal : PPO is a centralized DRL technique natively supported by ReinFog.}
elements.sent-2411-471: We choose it for its stable policy optimization approach that uses a clipped objective function to prevent excessive policy updates while maintaining high sample efficiency \cite{tang2020discretizing .}
elements.sent-2411-472: In our experiments, it uses Ornstein–Uhlenbeck noise exploration strategy and the reservoir sampling replay buffer, and its network structure introduces LSTMs.
elements.sent-2411-473: The DRL component placement algorithm is set to PSO.
elements.sent-2411-474: DQN \cite{mnih2015human : DQN is a centralized DRL technique natively supported by ReinFog.}
elements.sent-2411-475: We choose it as it is a fundamental and widely-adopted DRL technique that effectively combines Q-learning with DNNs through experience replay and target networks to stabilize training \cite{arulkumaran2017deep .}
elements.sent-2411-476: In our experiments, it uses $\epsilon$-greedy exploration strategy and the random sampling replay buffer, and its network structure introduces RNNs.
elements.sent-2411-477: The DRL component placement algorithm is set to GA.
elements.sent-2411-478: R2D2 \cite{kapturowski2018recurrent : R2D2 is a distributed DRL technique imported by ReinFog from the Ray library.}
elements.sent-2411-479: We choose it for its distributed architecture that extends DQN with RNNs to handle temporal dependencies while maintaining efficient parallel learning \cite{kapturowski2018recurrent .}
elements.sent-2411-480: In our experiments, the DRL component placement algorithm is set to MADCP.
elements.sent-2411-481: SAC \cite{haarnoja2018soft : SAC is a centralized DRL technique imported by ReinFog from the Ray library.}
elements.sent-2411-482: We choose it for its maximum entropy approach that provides automatic tuning to balance exploration and exploitation, leading to robust and stable learning performance \cite{haarnoja2018soft .}
elements.sent-2411-483: In our experiments, the DRL component placement algorithm is set to PSO.
elements.sent-2411-484: OHNSGA \cite{deng2021fogbus2 : OHNSGA is a GA-based meta-heuristic algorithm natively supported by FogBus2.}
elements.sent-2411-485: We extended this technique to support multi-objective optimization for comparison with DRL techniques.
elements.sent-2411-486: We conduct an extensive grid search to fine-tune the hyperparameters for each technique used in our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-487: The grid search involves systematically exploring various combinations of hyperparameters within predefined ranges to identify the optimal configuration for each technique.
elements.sent-2411-488: For the neural network architecture, following the guidance from \cite{glorot2010understanding and \cite{sun2019bert4rec , we experiment with different numbers of fully connected layers (ranging from 2 to 5) and various combinations of hidden layer units ({16, 32, 64, 128, 256 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-489: We find that a 3-layer architecture with [256, 256, 128] hidden units provided the best balance between model complexity and performance across most techniques.
elements.sent-2411-490: The choice of activation function can significantly impact the learning dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-491: While ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) is found to be effective for most techniques due to its non-linearity and reduced likelihood of vanishing gradients, we observe that TanH (Hyperbolic Tangent) works better for A3C, possibly due to its bounded output range.
elements.sent-2411-492: Following the guidance from \cite{espeholt2018impala , \cite{pmlr-v48-mniha16 , \cite{schulman2017proximal , \cite{hessel2018rainbow , and \cite{haarnoja2018soft , learning rates are tuned within the range of 0.0001 to 0.01, with most techniques performing optimally around 0.001.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-493: The discount factor, which balances immediate and future rewards, is tuned within the range of 0.8 to 0.99.
elements.sent-2411-494: Most techniques show optimal performance with a discount factor of 0.99, while A3C performed better with a slightly lower value of 0.9.
elements.sent-2411-495: For the OHNSGA algorithm, following the guidance from \cite{deb2002fast and \cite{deb2013evolutionary , which is not a DRL technique, we focus on tuning the population size and number of generations.}}
elements.sent-2411-496: The population size is varied from 50 to 500, and the number of generations is tested in the range of 50 to 200.
elements.sent-2411-497: After extensive experimentation, we find that a population size of 200 and 100 generations provides a good trade-off between solution quality and computational time.
elements.sent-2411-498: Table \ref{table:para presents the optimal hyperparameters identified for each technique.}
